coredata.dc:title: New insights into antifouling property and interfacial mechanism in photo-Fenton membrane distillation
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhenyu
Yan, Zhongsen
Chang, Haiqing
Wang, Qiankun
Liu, Fujian
Ni, Qichang
Xu, Junge
Liang, Heng
Qu, Fangshu
coredata.dc:description: Membrane distillation (MD), boasting high interception efficiency and low operational pressures, emerges as an innovative membrane technology. However, the occurrence of membrane fouling due to interaction between natural organic matter (NOM) and inorganic ions during the MD process curtails water purification efficiency, thereby constraining its potential applications. To address this quandary, this study integrates sulfate radical-based advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) into MD technology to bolster membrane fouling control. A straightforward hydrothermal method coupled with vacuum filtration was employed to synthesize a Co3O4/Nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots (NCDs)/PVDF (CN-PVDF) membrane for the first time, which was utilized in the MD treatment of simulated humic acid (HA) wastewater. Under visible light irradiation (1.9 kW/m2), CN-PVDF membrane activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) effectively altered the chemical attributes of the MD feed solution and reduced organic matter concentration. Moreover, it dismantled the carboxyl sites on HA that interact with Ca2+, consequently attenuating the formation of organic–inorganic complex pollutants. The XDLVO analysis showcased that photo-Fenton oxidation led to a diminishment in pollutant hydrophobicity, correlating with a 17.59 kT reduction in pollutant-membrane adsorption and a 7.47 kT amplification in adhesion barriers. This strategy transformed the initial two-stage fouling mode into a singular one, which significantly decreased the flux decline and the fouling layer thickness. Furthermore, the CN-PVDF membrane demonstrated self-cleaning capabilities via photo-Fenton. This study advances an innovative approach to bolster the fouling resistance of MD membranes and provides substantial theoretical support for the integration of SR-AOPs and MD technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Membrane distillation, Membrane fouling control
Self-cleaning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fouling resistance of membranes by photo-Fenton oxidation
Interfacial reaction and oxidation analysis by photo-Fenton oxidation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangzhou City-University Joint program for Basic Research
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52370069 , 52170072 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2023\u202F M734094 ), Fundamental Research Program of Guangzhou ( 2023B1515020057 ) and Guangzhou City-University Joint program for Basic Research ( 202201020172 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of photo-Fenton combined DCMD system.

SEM images depicting the cross-section of (a) CN-PVDF membrane and the surfaces of (b) Co3O4/PVDF and (c) CN-PVDF membranes, and STEM mappings of the CN-PVDF membrane showcasing (d) morphology, (e) overall elemental composition, along with detailed maps of (f) cobalt (Co), (g) oxygen (O), (h) carbon (C), and (i) nitrogen (N).

(a) Flux variation of PVDF membranes during various fouling resistance tests; (b) Hot spot map of flux curve fitted with complete blocking model where the slope k of the fitting line changes with the filtration stages (note: MAE stands for mean absolute error, i.e., the average of the absolute errors at each point).
SEM on both (left) surface and (right) cross section of PVDF membranes after (a-b) CN-PVDF + PMS, (c-d) PVDF + PMS, and (e-f) PVDF fouling resistance tests.
(a) Interaction energy (kT) between foulants treated by photo-Fenton and the surfaces of membranes, and (b-d) the variation of which change as the separation distance.

Chemical property of feed solution after different photo-Fenton treatments: (a) TOC and SUVA values; (b) absorption spectrum; (c) logarithmic transformation of absorption spectrum; (d) SR value; (e) Differential slope between different wavelength range; (f) FTIR result (note: PVDF, PVDF + PMS, and CN-PVDF + PMS stands for the feed solution without treatment, after ‘PVDF + PMS’ photo-Fenton treatment, and after ‘CN-PVDF + PMS’ photo-Fenton treatment, respectively).
Specific flux variation curve of four-cycle “fouling-cleaning” of CN-PVDF and PVDF membranes in the process of simulated HA wastewater treatment using DCMD.
Mechanistic diagram depicting the photo-Fenton reaction of the CN-PVDF membrane in the cleaning of membrane fouling.
Types of fouling models and their expressions.
The correlation coefficient R2 of four classical fouling models fitted to the flux curves in filtration stage Ⅰ (0–700 mL) and stage Ⅱ (700–900 mL) during different fouling resistance tests.

head.ce:title: New insights into antifouling property and interfacial mechanism in photo-Fenton membrane distillation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Membrane distillation (MD), boasting high interception efficiency and low operational pressures, emerges as an innovative membrane technology. However, the occurrence of membrane fouling due to interaction between natural organic matter (NOM) and inorganic ions during the MD process curtails water purification efficiency, thereby constraining its potential applications. To address this quandary, this study integrates sulfate radical-based advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) into MD technology to bolster membrane fouling control. A straightforward hydrothermal method coupled with vacuum filtration was employed to synthesize a Co3O4/Nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots (NCDs)/PVDF (CN-PVDF) membrane for the first time, which was utilized in the MD treatment of simulated humic acid (HA) wastewater. Under visible light irradiation (1.9 kW/m2), CN-PVDF membrane activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) effectively altered the chemical attributes of the MD feed solution and reduced organic matter concentration. Moreover, it dismantled the carboxyl sites on HA that interact with Ca2+, consequently attenuating the formation of organic–inorganic complex pollutants. The XDLVO analysis showcased that photo-Fenton oxidation led to a diminishment in pollutant hydrophobicity, correlating with a 17.59 kT reduction in pollutant-membrane adsorption and a 7.47 kT amplification in adhesion barriers. This strategy transformed the initial two-stage fouling mode into a singular one, which significantly decreased the flux decline and the fouling layer thickness. Furthermore, the CN-PVDF membrane demonstrated self-cleaning capabilities via photo-Fenton. This study advances an innovative approach to bolster the fouling resistance of MD membranes and provides substantial theoretical support for the integration of SR-AOPs and MD technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane distillation (MD), an emerging water treatment driven by vapor difference between feed and permeate sides, theoretically non-volatile matters could be entirely rejected during purification process [1]. However, the occurrence of membrane fouling in MD could impede mass transfer across the membrane [2]. Of particular concern is the fouling caused by natural organic matter (NOM) [3], such as humic acid (HA), which could be adsorbed onto the membrane surface through various mechanisms, including electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions [4]. Simultaneously, the calcium ion content in natural water bodies constitutes 50–70 % of the total cations [5]. This presence has the potential to bridge with HA, leading to the formation of a dense complexation fouling [6], substantially diminishing water treatment system efficiency, increasing energy usage, and raising maintenance expenses [7]. Hence, implementing effective measures for mitigating these adverse impacts is crucial.

In recent research, sulfate radical-based advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) involving highly reactive oxygen species, has demonstrated the capacity to degrade organic pollutants and alleviate membrane fouling [8]. Leveraging potent free radicals, these processes facilitate the conversion of organic pollutants into less harmful compounds, and even achieve complete mineralization to carbon dioxide and water [9]. Free radicals can be generated through activation of potassium mono-persulfate (PMS) or persulfate (PS) [10]. Notably, PMS, with its asymmetrical structure, exhibits heightened propensity for activation [11]. Predominant avenues of activation encompass electrochemistry, ultraviolet or heat irradiation, photocatalysis, and the activation of metal or non-metal catalysts [12]. Given that MD can harness industrial waste heat and solar energy [13], the amalgamation of thermally activated PMS and MD has piqued researchers' interest. Ding et al. [14] employed thermally activated PS pretreatment to mitigate membrane fouling in the real secondary effluent treatment via MD, yielding a 40.7 % total organic concentration (TOC) removal and effectively suppressing membrane fouling. Although the thermal activation exhibited a robust capacity to cleave unsaturated bonds in NOM, its subsequent mineralization proved to be inadequate, resulting in an ineffective TOC removal [15]. The efficacy of membrane fouling control in this context appeared to hinge on acidification induced by the Fenton-like reaction [16]. The deprotonation resulting from the pH reduction in the feed solution mitigated complex fouling. However, this acidification of the feed solution posed challenges for the sustained and stable operation of recirculation pumps and membranes in the MD system. To address this limitation and achieve effective membrane fouling control under wide operating conditions, there is a need for the activation of a more potent organic matter degradation mechanism.

Catalyst-mediated activation distinguishes itself by virtue of its prowess in efficient degradation against refractory pollutants, low energy consumption, and recyclability [17]. This type of activation, terms heterogeneous catalytic activation (HCA), which indicates that the phase forms of the catalyst and the reactant are different. HCA in traditional pressure-driven membrane applications has achieved notable success. For instance, the uniform dispersion of catalysts on the membrane surface can generate active substances, enhancing the resistance to membrane fouling and promoting the oxidative degradation of pollutants [18]. In a study by Bao et al. [19], a cobalt oxide porous functional ceramic membrane (CoCM) was developed. During the dead-end filtration of HA, free radicals generated in the CoCM facilitated the degradation of larger HA molecules into smaller ones, thereby reducing membrane pore blocking and the transmembrane pressure difference. Wang et al. [18] prepared a CuO-modified ceramic hollow fiber membrane, and through in-situ PMS activation, significantly mitigated membrane fouling in surface water treatment.

Nevertheless, the exploration of the combination of HCA and MD has been relatively limited, predominantly concentrating on the catalytic degradation and interception of volatile organic compounds (VOCs), such as aniline [20]. MD diverges from conventional pressure-driven membrane techniques, which employs hydrophobic membranes with larger pore sizes, allowing foulants on the membrane surface to experience high-temperature fluctuation-induced motion [21]. Consequently, the characteristics of membrane fouling formation in MD differ from those observed in other membrane technologies. Given these distinctions, studying the impact of HCA on membrane fouling control in the MD process is also worthwhile. Previously, our research team pioneered the photo-Fenton composite catalysts into MD fouling control, yielding a Co-MoS2/CNT/PTFE membrane [22]. This innovation demonstrated remarkable self-cleaning capabilities during the Rhodamine B wastewater treatment. However, in practical applications, membrane fouling is a complex phenomenon. For instance, Yan et al. [23] had identified organic–inorganic complex fouling, primarily complexes of HA with Ca2+, as the primary cause of severe membrane fouling in MD treatment of landfill leachate (LFL). The large and intricate structure of HA poses challenges for complete mineralization, potentially leading to the generation of smaller molecular weight organics during the oxidation process, which might exacerbate membrane fouling. In a study, the introduction of carboxyl groups during the Fenton oxidation process was found to strengthen the bridging between organic matter and Ca2+ [24]. Conversely, Zhu et al. [25] discovered that the hydrophobic portion of HA was preferentially degraded during oxidation, facilitating the weakening of the interaction force between HA and hydrophobic membranes. Currently, the control effect of HCA on complexation fouling in the MD process remains uncertain. Hence, it is essential to explore the impact of HCA on the physicochemical properties (hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity, functional group distribution, molecular weight size, etc.) of HA-Ca2+ complexes. This exploration aims to analyze changes in interaction patterns between foulants and membranes and assess the effect of HCA on controlling HA-induced complexation fouling during the MD process.

Within the realm of HCA, Co3O4 is one of the most promising catalytic materials due to its advantages such as abundant active sites, high chemical stability, and relatively low cost [26,27]. However, the performance of the traditional Co3O4 catalyst still has a large room for improvement, and it needs to be modified to meet the requirements of practical applications [28]. Due to the high stability, electrical conductivity and synergism of carbon-based materials, the combination of carbon-based materials with other materials to form composite materials can enhance the reactive active site and promote electron transfer between PMS and catalysts [29]. Illustratively, Peng et al. [30] synthesized a novel catalyst Co/CNTs for promoting the activation of PMS to degrade methyl parabens, while Dang et al. [31] successfully prepared reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/Co3O4 to enhance the reaction rate between PMS and pollutants. In sum, cobalt-based and carbon-based composites furnishes an efficacious catalyst for PMS activation.

In this study, a novel Co3O4/Nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots (NCDs)/PVDF (CN-PVDF) membrane was synthesized. The resulting membrane was subsequently employed for the treatment of simulated organic wastewater in direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD). Comprehensive assessments of the membrane encompassed an evaluation of its resistance against membrane fouling and its self-cleaning efficacy under visible light exposure and PMS stimulation. Furthermore, a deeper comprehension of the variation in physicochemical characteristics of foulants and the interaction mechanism between foulants and the membrane was pursued.

Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane was purchased from Millipore Co. (USA). HA, PMS (KHSO5·0.5KHSO4·0.5K2SO4), glycerin, and diiodomethane were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Calcium chloride, urea, citric acid, and cobalt nitrate hexahydrate were all purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. (China). All the above chemicals were analytically pure (A.R.). Besides, Nafion solution D520 was gained from Dupont China Group Co.

The CN-PVDF membrane was fabricated by hydrothermal reaction and filtration loading method, which were convenient to replicate commercially. Specific CN-PVDF membrane fabrication process was shown in Text S1 (SI).

The microstructure of the CN-PVDF and membrane fouling was characterized through scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Verios G4, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., USA), preceded by both drying and a gold spraying process, yielding a gold layer approximately 10 nm thick. To ascertain the element distribution across the CN-PVDF membrane, scanning transmission electron microscopy-mapping (STEM-mapping, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI Co., USA) was employed, enabling the collection of distinctive elemental signals. The molecular structure and chemical attributes of the fouling layer adhered to the membrane surface were discerned via Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet is50, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., USA) characterization, with the test scope spanning from 400 to 4000 cm−1.

The feed solution is simulated HA wastewater containing 500 mg/L HA and 1 mM CaCl2 (pH = 7.0). The concentration ratio between HA and Ca2+ has a great influence on the formation of membrane fouling. For the specific determination process of wastewater composition, see Text S2 (SI).

Schematic diagram of photo-Fenton combined DCMD unit is shown in Fig. 1 . This set-up is a modification of the previous DCMD device [32], where a 500 W tungsten iodine lamp installed above the original membrane module to simulate sun light intensity.

Membrane fouling resistance experiment after photo-Fenton oxidation was set to test the oxidation and antifouling properties of CN-PVDF membrane. In this test, prior to commencing the DCMD procedure, 50 mM PMS was introduced into the simulated HA wastewater. This PMS-laden solution was meticulously blended to ensure its uniform integration into the feed circulation system. Subsequent activation was initiated by activating the light source, fostering a catalytic interaction between the 25 cm2 membranes (PVDF or CN-PVDF) and the PMS present in the system. Following a reaction duration of 30 min, the light source was turned off. The residual PMS was quenched by 5 mM sodium bisulfite, and the pH of the solution was readjusted to 7.0. According to the different membrane used, it was divided into two experimental groups PVDF + PMS and CN-PVDF + PMS, and blank group (named PVDF group) was set without this pretreatment. With the PVDF membrane in module, the DCMD water production experiment was then initiated. The water production process was halted upon reaching a cumulative output of 900 mL. Throughout the DCMD process, alterations in permeate weight values were diligently monitored and recorded.

“Fouling-cleaning” cycle experiment was set up to evaluate the photo-Fenton cleaning performance and application potential of the CN-PVDF membrane. In this test, simulated HA wastewater was treated by DCMD until the permeate volume achieve 240 mL. After that, the solution in the feeding circulation system was replaced with 1 L aqueous solution containing 5 mM PMS, and light was turned on to irradiate the pollutants deposited on the membrane (PVDF and CN-PVDF) surface for 30 min. The whole stage was set as a cycle of operation, by comparing the increase of specific flux during the photo-Fenton cleaning process, to analyze the cleaning efficiency of the CN-PVDF membrane. Besides, four cycles of operation were conducted to evaluate the operation stability of CN-PVDF membrane.
Membrane fouling behavior was evaluated by membrane flux variation, fouling model, and its fitted hot spot map.

The calculation of membrane flux can be obtained by the ratio of the actual volume or mass of the membrane to the effective area of the membrane and the filtration time during the membrane separation process, and the specific calculation formula is as follows:
(1)
J =
V
A · t
where J is membrane flux (L/m2·h, LMH); V is the filtration volume (L); A is the effective membrane area (m2); t is the filtration time (h).

By connecting the real-time membrane flux value at each time of membrane filtration with the initial flux, the concept of specific flux is derived, which more intuitively reflects the degree of membrane flux decline, as shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
J ′
=
J i
J 0
where Ji is the membrane flux value (LMH) at time i, and J0 is the initial flux value (LMH).

Membrane fouling model provides a good theoretical basis for membrane fouling in membrane distillation process, which includes four classical types, including complete blocking, standard blocking, middle blocking, and cake filtration model [33]. These four fouling models can be summarized by formula (3) [34]:
(3)
d 2
t
d
V 2
= K
(
dt
dV
)
n

where V is the permeate volume (cm3) and K is the model coefficient. To further integrate and simplify, four different types of membrane fouling models can present different performance equations through different characteristic index n values [35], see Table 1 for details.

The correlation coefficient of fitting curve was scrutinized to establish the optimal fouling model. Guided by this model, the DCMD water production process was categorized into five stages, facilitating a hotpot map of the dynamic evolution of membrane fouling throughout the process.

The hydrophobicity of the pollutants was determined by specific ultraviolet absorbance (SUVA). SUVA is equal to the ratio of UV254 to total organic carbon (TOC), and the SUVA value can reflect the hydrophobicity of the solution [36]. The larger the calculated SUVA value, the stronger the hydrophobicity of the solution.

The three-dimensional fluorometer FS5 (Edinburgh Co., United Kingdom) was used to conduct transmission scanning of foulants in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm, and then the absorption spectrum of water samples in the whole wavelength range was obtained by combining it with the transmission spectrum of ultra-pure water. Through logarithmic change processing of the spectrum, the values of S275-295 and SR can be calculated, which can be used to reflect the change of NOM molecular weight, to conduct semi-quantitative characterization. The specific calculation process is as follows [37].

a is the absorption coefficient of NOM, as shown in Eq. (4):
(4)
a = 2.303 ×
U
V λ
D
where UVλ is the UV absorbance of the sample at wavelength λ nm; D is the dilution multiple of the solution.

The value of SR involves the absorbance intensity value in two wavelength ranges (275–295 nm and 350–400 nm), which more accurately and comprehensively reflects the relevant parameters of water quality than UV254. It is generally believed that the larger the value of S275-295 and SR , the smaller the molecular weight of NOM. The specific calculation formula is shown in Eq. (5):
(5)
S R
=
S
275 - 295
S
350 - 400

where S275-295 is the slope of the linear fitting equation of log(a) in the range of wavelength 275–295 nm; S350-400 is the slope of the linear fitting equation for log(a) in the wavelength range 350–400 nm; log(a) is the result of the logarithmic change of the change curve of the absorption coefficient a.

Differential logarithmic transformation absorption spectroscopy (DLAS) was used to reflect the degree of complexation between dissolved organics and metal cations [33]. DLAS in this study is defined as the difference of the slope of the log-transformed absorption spectrum in a certain wavelength range between the oxidized solution and the reference solution (non-oxidized solution), which is expressed by the formula as a previous study [38].

The process of membrane fouling deposition and adhesion is governed by the intricate thermodynamic interactions occurring at close proximity between the contaminant and the membrane surface [39,40]. These interactions are effectively quantifiable through the utilization of the Expanded Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek (XDLVO) theory [41]. Within this theoretical framework, the interaction energy existing between the contaminant and the membrane material is comprised of three distinct components: attractive van der Waals forces (AB), electrostatic interactions (EL), and repulsive steric interactions (LW). The detailed calculation process of the interaction energy between foulants and membrane surface is shown in Text S3.

The SEM and STEM-mapping of the CN-PVDF membrane are shown in Fig. 2 . Co3O4 was comprised of voluminous particles varying in size (Fig. 2(a)), amalgamating into an irregular structure of approximately 1 μm in dimension. Facilitated by the binding influence of the Nafion solution, these particles established intimate interconnections, substantiating the successful incorporation of the Co3O4 material. Upon integration of NCDs into Co3O4, numerous diminutive particles amassed amidst the larger, solid structures, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The substance in the blue box of Fig. 2(b) is NCDs, and NCDs liquid presented fluorescence characteristic under ultraviolet irradiation, which is the main characteristic of carbon quantum dots.

STEM-mapping analysis of CN-PVDF membrane revealed discernible signals corresponding to the Co, O, C, and N elements (Fig. 2(d)). The distribution patterns of Co and O elements exhibited remarkable congruence, consistent with the composition of Co3O4. Simultaneously, similar distribution densities and ranges of signal points were observed in Fig. 2(g) and (h), which are attributed to the presence of NCDs. In contrast to Co and O elements, fewer signal points were attributed to N and C elements, aligning with the doping ratio of Co3O4 to NCDs (100:1) employed in the fabrication of Co3O4/NCDs materials. Therefore, it could be inferred that Co3O4/NCDs particles was successfully loaded on the PVDF membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fouling resistance of membranes by photo-Fenton oxidation

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) illustrates the permeate performance of PVDF membranes in various fouling resistance tests. The membrane exhibited an initially low flux in PVDF group, which experienced a rapid decline as the filtration volume reached 700 mL, indicating a two-stage flux decline model. Approximately 32 % of the flux was lost by the end of the PVDF and PVDF + PMS groups. In contrast, the PVDF membrane in CN-PVDF + PMS group demonstrated a gradual and consistently small decline in flux, with a loss of less than 10 % in total flux by the end, showcasing the effectiveness of the CN-PVDF membrane in mitigating HA-Ca2+ membrane fouling. In addition, the conductivity of the permeate water remained around 10 μS/cm for each group, and no membrane wetting was observed.

As depicted in Table 2 , for the PVDF group, there was a significant difference in membrane fouling behavior between the two stages, with the early stages of operation closer to the standard and middle fouling models. Conventionally, standard and middle fouling mechanisms are perceived to transpire within the membrane pores, leading to intricate cleaning and irreversible fouling [42]. As water production proceeded, the pollutants were concentrated on the membrane surface and the fouling pattern shifted toward cake filtration. The membrane fouling in the process of complete blocking and cake filtration mainly occurs on the membrane surface, which is easily recovered by hydraulic washing [43]. Following the photo-Fenton oxidation of the PVDF and the CN-PVDF membranes, the fit degree between the flux curve and the four fouling models increased significantly. At this situation, the foulants in feed solution contained HA-Ca2+ complexes, oxidized HA, and unoxidized HA. Besides, the degradation products of HA were very diverse, with organic matter of different sizes and molecular weights present. A study had previously found that [44] the hydrodynamic size of HA was significantly altered upon oxidation, while the unsaturated bonds during oxidation tend to cause aggregation to form a conjugated structure, resulting in the formation of larger flocs [45]. The complexity of the pollutant sizes after the photo-Fenton reaction led to this composite fouling behavior, i.e., all four fouling modes came into play.

Owing to the alignment between the flux curves and four fouling models of each group was close, a hot pot map illustrating the k value of fitting curves for five stages of water production was constructed under one of models (in the case of complete blocking model), which was performed to scrutinize the dynamic evolution of membrane fouling behavior [46], as presented in Fig. 3(b). The trend observed in the heat maps for both the PVDF group and the PVDF + PMS group exhibited similarity. In these cases, the k slope demonstrated an ascending trajectory in conjunction with escalating infiltration volume. This phenomenon corresponded to the earlier-identified “two-stage” fouling pattern evident in the preceding flux curves. In such circumstances, during the initial operational phase, the membrane flux diminished gradually, but subsequently, the rate of flux decline experienced a pronounced escalation upon reaching a critical point. It could be hypothesized that at this juncture, a fouling gel layer formed comprehensively across the membrane surface, and the effective water-producing membrane surface area contracted significantly. In contrast, the flux decline was solely expedited during the initial (0–180 mL) and intermediate (360–540 mL) stages of the DCMD operation in CN-PVDF + PMS group. Remarkably, the overall flux remained minimally impacted by this treatment.

After conducting the fouling resistance experiments, an analysis of fouling morphology and membrane thickness was performed through SEM imaging, with specific findings presented in Fig. 4 . As depicted in Fig. 4(a), the membrane exhibited a limited coverage of pollutants, allowing the pores of PVDF membrane to remain distinctly visible. Conversely, a dense filter-cake-like fouling layer was evident in both the PVDF group and the PVDF + PMS experimental group (depicted in Fig. 4(c) and (e)). In these cases, the membrane surface became fully obscured by pollutants, and the accumulation of both powdery and rod-like particles was also observed. This cohesive fouling layer engendered heightened mass transfer resistance and might contribute to temperature difference polarization [33]. Consequently, the vapor flux during DCMD operation was diminished. This phenomenon accounted for the most substantial drop in flux within such two experimental groups, as illustrated in Fig. 3(a).

Upon examining cross-sectional views of the membranes, it became apparent that the interaction between the CN-PVDF membrane and PMS led to a reduction in the thickness of fouling layer from 2.63 μm to 0.38 μm, which further substantiated the antifouling efficacy by oxidation effect of the CN-PVDF membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial reaction and oxidation analysis by photo-Fenton oxidation

ce:section.ce:para: The XDLVO theory was employed to analyze the interaction forces between the pollutants and the membrane following photo-Fenton oxidation, aiming to assess the propensity for membrane fouling (Fig. 5 ). As the calculated EL interaction energy was notably smaller by approximately 6–7 orders of magnitude in comparison to the AB and LW interaction energies, the EL free energy was ignored (More details are shown in Text S3). Throughout this analysis, interface interaction energies between all pollutants and membranes exhibited negative values, indicative of mutual attraction between the two entities.

In the absence of PMS, the strongest adhesion occurred between the foulant and the PVDF membrane, with an adhesion tendency of −51.59 kT. Interestingly, this propensity remained unchanged despite PMS activation. Yet, upon PMS activation by CN-PVDF membrane, the AB force notably decreased, lowering the total interaction energy to around −34 kT. The AB free energy dominates the composition of the total free energy, and the oxidation process could profoundly alter the AB free energy of HA and the membrane surface, consequently diminishing the binding propensity between the two. This phenomenon was consistent with findings reported by [45] in their investigation utilizing a CuFe2O4-tailored ceramic membrane for the oxidation of HA. In addition, after photo-Fenton oxidation, the interaction modes within the foulants all changed from mutual attraction to mutual repulsion, especially in the CN-PVDF + PMS experimental group, where the interaction energy increased from −2.32 KT to 14.72 kT (see Fig. S3). This may be related to the hydration repulsion formed by hydrated cations around the surface of negatively charged particles, which would change the water structure around the particles, thus forming a hydration barrier against particle aggregation [6].

The variation of interaction energy with separation distance is shown in Fig. 5(b)–(d). As the pollutants approached the membrane surface within a range of 0–10 nm, a significant interface interaction emerged, beginning to exert control over membrane fouling tendencies. Among the interface interaction energies, UAB assumed a pivotal role. Notably, within the 0–2.5 nm interval, the attractive forces governed by UAB displayed a swift escalation as the separation distance decreased. Across all experimental groups, ULW consistently held the lowest value, only exhibiting slight modulation when the separation distance neared 0 nm. While the UAB value demonstrated substantial potency, its attenuation lessened with expanding separation distances, in contrast to the slower decline observed for UEL. Consequently, UAB exhibited the capability to govern membrane fouling behavior primarily within a restricted range of separation distances. On the other hand, the impact of UEL impact extended across a broader spectrum of separation distances, contributing to its ability to influence membrane fouling dynamics over a more expansive range.

UTOT represents the cumulative outcome of the other three curves, demonstrating distinct trends in relation to separation distance alterations. As the separation distance increases, UTOT initially experienced a rapid rise then followed by a gradual decline. The pinnacle value at this juncture signifies the energy barrier, representing the threshold pollutants must surpass to adhere to the membrane surface. The energy barriers manifested at separation distances of 3.52, 3.49, and 2.83 nm, corresponding to (b), (c), and (d) in Fig. 5, respectively. The energy barrier for non-photo-Fenton oxidized pollutants registered at 9.12 kT, the lowest level. This low value indicated that the barrier these pollutants need to overcome was minimal, rendering them more prone to adhering to the membrane surface. Following photo-Fenton oxidation, the energy barrier underwent different increments. Of note, the catalytic impact of the CN-PVDF membrane catalysis brought about a substantial effect, elevating the energy barrier to 16.59 kT. This resulted in curtailing the affinity of foulants for the membrane surface and effectively mitigating the development of fouling layers during the simulated HA wastewater treatment via DCMD.

Fig. 6 illustrates the evolution in the chemical properties of the feed solution post various photo-Fenton oxidation processes. Following photo-Fenton oxidation facilitated by the CN-PVDF membrane, a noteworthy transformation was observed. The TOC content of the feed solution was decreased substantially, plummeting from 195.5 to 39.8 mg/L, signifying an impressive mineralization rate of approximately 80 % of the organic matter. This outcome might be attributed to the catalytic impact of active free radicals, which induce the integration of hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., ·OH) into the structure of HA [47]. This, in turn, initiates a cascade of events involving the depolymerization and mineralization of HA [25]. With the effective mineralization of HA, the ratio of Ca2+ to HA concentration in the feed solution experienced a significant increase. This alteration might result in a more pronounced change in hydration repulsion compared to the reduction in electrostatic repulsion [48], thereby aiding in diminishing the adhesion between HA-HA and HA-PVDF and promoting the formation of a less rigid HA fouling layer [49]. Furthermore, a significant reduction in the SUVA value was also noted, dwindling from 7.06 to 4.31 L/g·m, which underscored the efficient degradation of hydrophobic components and elimination of aromatic structures within HA [50]. The degradation of these components proved advantageous, as it served to attenuate the hydrophobic interactions between foulants and the PVDF membrane.

Simultaneously, the absorbance of feed solutions was analyzed in the visible range (Fig. 6(b)). The characteristic HA peak (254 nm) exhibited varying shifts post photo-Fenton oxidation. Notably, the most significant shift was observed within the CN-PVDF + PMS group, substantially weakening the absorbance. This demonstrated that the oxidation method could efficiently alter the molecular components within HA, leading to the breakdown of highly unsaturated HA and, consequently, facilitating the effective degradation of UV254 and TOC. Additionally, the absorbance was translated into light absorption coefficient, followed by logarithmic transformation for molecular weight analysis (Fig. 6(c) and (d)). Within the 275–290 nm wavelength span, the absolute slope of the log(a) value fitting line registered as 0.00128, 0.00197, and 0.00717 for the PVDF, PVDF + PMS, and CN-PVDF + PMS experimental groups, respectively. The ascending order of SR values in Fig. 6(d) corresponded, with the highest SR value found in CN-PVDF + PMS group at 1.431. The dominant factor governing the optical properties of NOM was the charge transfer energy within the NOM molecule [51]. The reduction in NOM molecular weight modified intramolecular charge interactions, shifting the absorption spectrum toward shorter wavelengths [37]. This resulted in steep log(a) curves and larger S275-295 values within the lower absorbance range (275–295 nm), consequently leading to increased SR values. Thus, it might be deduced that following CN-PVDF membrane photo-Fenton oxidation, the covalent bond of HA underwent oxidation and cleavage [52], disintegrating its macro-molecular structure and transforming it into lower molecular weight organic matter. Ding et al. [14] found that the weight average molecular weight (MWw) strength of CHO, CH and CHN compounds decreased significantly during the treatment of NOM by thermal activation PS. In general, compounds with lower molecular weight are more likely to be adsorbed on the membrane surface, which is not conducive to the control of membrane fouling. However, in the process of membrane treatment, the phenomenon of membrane fouling is also affected by the concentration of organic matter, the conformation of organic matter molecules, and the complexation reaction [14].

The DLAS-derived differential slope, presented in Fig. 6(e), served as a measure to assess the level of complexation within the solution. After subjecting the CN-PVDF membrane to oxidation treatment, a discernible reduction in the formation of organic–inorganic complexes within the feed solution was observed. This substantiated the effective degradation of HA during the photo-Fenton process, leading to a decrease in the number of binding sites between Ca2+ and organic matter. This might be due to the breakdown of the HA chain structure, which lowered the organic matter from connecting to Ca2+ [15]. Consequently, the propensity for complex formation was weakened. For further analysis, three kinds of solutions were loaded onto PVDF membranes, subsequently subjected to FTIR characterization, as illustrated in Fig. 6(f). Since the intensity signals within the wavelength range of 500–1500 cm−1 was produced by PVDF membranes, the span of 1500–4000 cm−1 was highlighted for comparative analysis [23]. Notably, the HA solution exhibited a pronounced broad peak at 3380 cm−1, possibly corresponding to the presence of O-H groups [53]. Furthermore, a noticeable stretching at 1578 cm−1 emerged, primarily attributed to the CC chemical bond. Following oxidation treatment, the characteristic peak at 1578 cm−1 experienced a shift, concurrent with the appearance of a stretch at 1718 cm−1. This might be attributed to the fracture of carboxyl groups, yielding CO groups during the degradation process [54]. In broad terms, the oxidation process typically involves the oxygenation of organic matter [14], leading to an increase in the proportion of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as –COOH. Nevertheless, owing to the potent organic matter degradation achieved by the CN-PVDF membrane (with a mineralization rate reaching 80 %), the overall organic matter content was significantly diminished. Consequently, the total concentration of carboxyl groups was relatively low, limiting the available sites for complexation reactions.

In practical applications, the volume of organic wastewater requiring treatment is substantial. If photo-Fenton oxidation is implemented prior to DCMD operation, it might necessitate a significant PMS dosage, potentially resulting in elevated operational costs. The online photo-Fenton cleaning following the end of water production may represent a more practical approach. As shown in Fig. 7 , a four-cycle “fouling-cleaning” test was conducted to dissect the photo-Fenton cleaning performance and application potential of CN-PVDF membrane. Throughout the four-cycle test, the CN-PVDF membrane exhibited enhanced water production efficiency in contrast to the PVDF membrane. Notably, the initial specific flux values during the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th cycles stood at 0.97, 0.93, and 0.91, respectively, all surpassing the 0.9 threshold. Conversely, the specific discharge capacity of the PVDF membrane during the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th operating cycles obviously dwindled, recording values of 0.81, 0.74, and 0.66, respectively. Notably, the CN-PVDF membrane consistently attained effective flux recovery within each cycle during the process of photo-Fenton cleaning, amounting to an approximate 0.22 flux recovery degree. In contrast, the PVDF membrane exhibited minimal flux enhancement during the initial photo-Fenton cleaning cycle. The fouling of PVDF membrane was primarily driven by the force of interaction between the foulants and the membrane in the initial cycle experiment. During this phase, the mutual adhesion force peaked at its highest level (−51.59 kT), resulting in a mere 0.02 recovery of the specific flux during cleaning. Subsequent cycle experiments, however, witnessed the presence of the original fouling layer, causing the further development of membrane fouling to rely more on the interaction force among the foulants. This force was relatively low (−2.32 kT), rendering the following membrane fouling easier to clean. As the “fouling-cleaning” experiment progressed, the divergence between the specific flux of the PVDF membrane and the CN-PVDF membrane progressively widened. This divergence suggested that the interfacial oxidation and enhancement of cleaning effect was achieved by CN-PVDF membrane, and it was a testament to the stability of the material loading during CN-PVDF membrane operation and its enduring performance effect over the long-term. Moreover, the conductivity of the permeate water remained almost constant (around 20 μS/cm) throughout these two cycle tests, indicating an absence of membrane wetting. In sum, this kind of photo-Fenton membrane might achieve efficient wastewater treatment by pre-oxidation or online cleaning, possessing great application potential.

Fig. 8 illustrates the mechanistic diagram depicting the photo-Fenton reaction of the CN-PVDF membrane in the cleaning of membrane fouling. To clearly illustrate the mechanism, the mechanism explanation is structured into three parts, mirroring the decomposition and desorption stages of membrane fouling, which is as follows. (1) reduction of complexes in the membrane fouling layer: during the photo-Fenton process, there was a notable decrease in the volume of carboxylate within the HA, indicating the disruption of Ca2+ binding sites within the HA structure. Consequently, this led to a significant reduction in the overall presence of Ca2+-HA complexes, the primary constituents of the membrane fouling layer. This observation aligns with a study by Guo et al. [15], where pre-treatment with thermally activated PDS disrupted the structure of humic substances within NOM, thereby reducing their affinity to bind with valuable cations and decreasing the likelihood of valuable cation deposition on the membrane surface. (2) alteration of interaction within foulants: following the oxidation process, a portion of HA on the membrane surface was mineralized, while the remaining macromolecular structure was partially degraded. This resulted in an increased concentration ratio of Ca2+ to HA in the feed solution, potentially fostering hydration repulsion among foulants [48]. Consistent with the findings of the XDLVO theory (Fig. S3), interactions within the foulants transitioned from mutual attraction to mutual repulsion after photo-Fenton oxidation. Consequently, substances within the fouling layer began dispersing, thereby increasing the effective reaction area upon foulants, and facilitating further decomposition of foulants. (3) reduction of mutual attraction between foulants and membrane: moreover, this process led to a notable decrease in the SUVA value of HA, indicating a decrease in the hydrophobicity of pollutants. Yu et al. [55] also observed that hydrophobic organic matter in NOM converted to hydrophilic forms during the photocatalytic membrane reaction. Consequently, the mutual attraction between foulants and the membrane surface diminished, facilitating the desorption of foulants from the membrane surface.

In summary, the photo-Fenton cleaning process altered foulant interactions, promoting mutual repulsion, thus decreasing Ca2+-HA complexes, and decomposing the fouling layer. Additionally, it reduced attraction between foulants and the membrane surface, aiding in the desorption of the fouling layer. Through this multifaceted approach, moderate success was achieved in mitigating membrane fouling.

In this study, the utilization of photo-Fenton technology for treating simulated HA wastewater through DCMD was explored. Additionally, a comprehensive investigation was conducted to understand the efficacy of membrane fouling control and its underlying mechanisms. The key findings are summarized as follows:

(1) The photo-Fenton treatment significantly diminished the molecular weight, hydrophobicity, and concentration of organic matter in simulated HA wastewater, which notably decreased the binding sites of carboxyl groups on HA that interact with Ca2+, culminating in a decrease in the formation of organic–inorganic complexes during DCMD operation.

(2) The photo-Fenton process contributed to reducing the mutual attraction between foulants and the PVDF membrane, which engendered a decline in adhesion force from 51.59 mJ/cm2 to 34.01 mJ/cm2, coupled with an elevation in the energy barrier by 7.47 kT, subsequently tempering the propensity for membrane fouling formation.

(3) The combination of photo-Fenton with DCMD technology demonstrated a pronounced efficacy in mitigating membrane fouling stemming from simulated HA wastewater. Notably, the prevailing two-stage fouling mode underwent transformation into a single-stage composite mode, resulting in a substantial reduction of approximately 2.63 μm in the thickness of the membrane fouling layer.

(4) The photo-Fenton membrane exhibited interface self-cleaning capabilities after the treatment of simulated HA wastewater using DCMD, as evidenced by the specific flux rebounding from 0.76 to 0.97 after the cleaning process.

Zhenyu Lu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Zhongsen Yan: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Haiqing Chang: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qiankun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Fujian Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Qichang Ni: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Junge Xu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Heng Liang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Fangshu Qu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
